  "answer": "A **hard link** is an additional directory entry pointing to the same inode (the actual file data) as the original file. All hard links to a file are indistinguishable from the original; if you delete the original name, the data remains accessible through any remaining hard link. Hard links cannot cross filesystem boundaries and cannot link to directories.\n\nA **symbolic link** (symlink) is a special file that contains a path to another file or directory. It's like a shortcut. If you delete the target file, the symlink becomes broken (dangling). Symlinks can point to files on different filesystems and can link to directories.\n\nKey differences:\n- **Inode**: Hard links share the same inode; symlinks have their own inode.\n- **Filesystem**: Hard links must be on the same filesystem; symlinks can cross filesystems.\n- **Directories**: Hard links cannot link to directories (with rare exceptions); symlinks can.\n- **Deletion**: Deleting the original file leaves hard links intact; symlinks become broken.\n- **Size**: Hard links don't consume extra disk space for data; symlinks consume space for the path string.",
